title: One thing I love about this solo Dungeon Crawl (Dungeon Crusade - Book 1: Genesis of Evil)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lADjNtrJtIo

A short-form appreciation — Daniel (@dungeondive) singles out a single feature he keeps coming back to in **Dungeon Crusade**: the random encounter deck.

His broader argument is that an encounter deck or table is the line between a great dungeon crawl and a good one — it sits in every game in his (forthcoming) top-ten dungeon-crawl list, because it is what stops every turn from feeling like every other turn. Dungeon Crusade ships with around eighty cards, of which thirty are drawn into a given game's encounter deck, plus ten **interludes** that throttle the pace and let the heroes breathe (with their own little narrative flavour text — empty barbaric-prison chambers, flickering torchlit corridors).

The remaining cards split into three subgroups. **Environments** persist on the board until replaced — chilling air that costs every hero two movement, distant war drums that make minion movement +4, a glow that turns d3 mining yields into d6. **Events** are one-offs — randomly select a hero, a battered cobwebbed chest, four lockpicking attempts, a d8 trap if you blow the last try; or a hero looks up to find four cu'an bats coming for him. **Challenges** are usually bad and usually clever — a sub-dungeon ladder leading down into three rolled-on-d6 chambers, four archaic coffins drawn one die at a time, a chamber of dormant skeletons with a strength-19 dig test that ends in a shattered green potion if you fail. The deck is what keeps the dungeon alive.

----

Daniel's claim is that an encounter deck is the cleanest signal of a top-tier dungeon crawl. Which game's encounter deck would *you* point to as the gold standard — and which excellent crawl gets away without one?
